label: analysis

text: The New York Knicks are only the third team in NBA history to win the NBA Championship without a player who received MVP votes for the regular season, joining the 77-78 Washington Bullets and the 67-68 Celtics.
label: analysis

text: Even the 2004 Pistons, who many point to as an outlier in having no stars, had Ben Wallace receiving MVP votes and going on to finish 7th overall in MVP voting.
label: analysis

text: I think this says more about MVP voting this year than it does about Brunson or the Knicks. Only 8 players received votes this year, which is the lowest since 68-69. NBA media is in an echo chamber.
label: analysis

text: Jokic, SGA, Wemby, and Luka are all clearly top 4 generational talents in their primes. You don't deserve your vote if you didn't put them in the top 4 based on regular season stats and record. So it's not surprising only 4 other players received votes.
label: hot_take

text: This years voting was really just about who you put fifth. Brunson would have been an OK option but if you're honest and don't let recency bias cloud your view, Brunson did not have a better regular season than Brown or Cade.
label: analysis

text: In a span of 6 minutes in the 4th quarter of Game 5, Brunson scored 13 straight points and 15 of 17 to take the Knicks from a 10 point deficit with 8 minutes remaining to a 2 point lead with 1 minute remaining. Up there with all-time championship-clinching stretches like Jordan in the 1998 closeout game.
label: analysis

text: Brunson a winner at every level. He took the pay-cut, managed his way through the regular season and cranked up when there was something real to play for. He has taken three of the four MVP trophies available this postseason.
label: analysis

text: In 1969, rookie Wes Unseld stole the MVP from Willis Reed despite only getting 68% of the ROTY vote. The next year he was only 8th in MVP voting and not an All-Star nor All-NBA.
label: analysis

text: The 77-78 Bullets went through a gauntlet against the Iceman, Dr J, and Sikma. They deserve more flowers for their win.
label: analysis

text: Luka became a distant third in MVP voting in 2024 despite being borderline superhuman that season. That made it pretty clear most voters do not actually watch a lot of games.
label: analysis

text: Looks like all the bigs chose Wemby and all the perimeter players chose perimeter defenders. Chet only got one vote too.
label: analysis

text: Wemby and Gobert are obviously better defenders than Chet and also play center. Even if a voter most valued the specific skillset Chet brings, they would end up rating him as the third best defender. It's hard to imagine the analysis that would lead to anyone ranking Chet number 1.
label: analysis

text: When Chet was on the court this season 24.26% of opponents shots were at the rim and they'd only shoot 57.84% at the rim. When he was off the court that FG% rises to 64.31%. When Gobert was on the court opponents shot 63.48% at the rim, and when he was off the court that stayed about the same at 63.3%.
label: analysis

text: Rudy Gobert is a four-time DPOY who should probably have five, and just had arguably a top 3 defensive impact season of his career at age 33, and real human beings will tell you he's not good simply because they don't like him.
label: analysis

text: I imagine a lot of ball handlers vote based on who they view as the best 1 on 1 defenders at the point of attack. In the current NBA those guys will never be as impactful as rim defenders because teams just force switches to preferred matchups.
label: analysis

text: Players don't pick Gobert because he's not a great 1 on 1 defender when he gets baited out, but he's great when you funnel everyone towards him. Whereas Wemby, you go past him and you still get your ass blocked after sidestepping.
label: analysis

text: Only 41% is honestly lowkey crazy. A ton of the names taking up a share on this list aren't really close enough to be considered better defenders than Wemby. 12 blocks is insane even accounting for some goaltending calls.
label: analysis

text: Rudy is still the 2nd best defender on this list. He shut down Jokic and is trying to carry that momentum to Wemby and the Spurs. He's been what's gotten the Wolves to the second round on defense.
label: analysis

text: Being a giant human being is by far the most important part of defense — stopping shots at the rim or keeping offense away from the rim so wing defenders can commit to hard perimeter defense. Rudy, Chet, and Wemby just by being enormous do that on top of being very good at what they do.
label: analysis

text: Chet on the perimeter — when Chet is on the court opponents shoot 35.7% from 3 vs 37.1% when off. Gobert on/off for opponent 3pt shooting is 35% on and 35.3% off. Chet is mobile enough to get out to corners and contest while still protecting the rim.
label: analysis

text: Playoff experience is huge at this time of the year. In recent years: 25 Rockets was the second seed and was a first round exit, 24 Thunder was the first seed and was a second round exit, 23 Kings was the third seed and was a first round exit, 22 Grizzlies was the second seed and was a first round exit.
label: analysis

text: There is basically no example of a team winning when their best player hasn't played a playoff game. Not Larry, not Cousy, not Russell, not Jordan, not LeBron, not Kareem. Closest was Wilt who was league MVP in his rookie season and lost in the east coast finals.
label: analysis

text: The problem with the experience argument is that the better players usually have had played in the playoffs anyway, so it doesn't really have to do with playoff experience as opposed to how good the player themselves are.
label: analysis

text: Post all star break, here are the 3pt percentages of Spurs players: Dylan Harper 47.9%, Devin Vassell 43.5%, Barnes 43.0%, Castle 41.7%, Champagnie 40.7%. Despite not being great from 3, Fox, KJ and Wemby still have good FG% at 46.9%, 51.4%, and 49.1%.
label: analysis

text: The Spurs were something like 28 and 0 when they shot 40% from distance. When they're hitting their shots they're literally unbeatable. When they're not you can crash the paint and make things difficult for their slashing base attack.

text: I think their downfall is their 3 point shooting more than experience. They have fake shooters more than the experience factor being a problem.

text: Basketball reference playoff odds has them at 27% to make the finals. That seems slightly high. If they get past the Blazers it's a coin flip against Denver and against OKC more like 60/40.

text: Wemby competed against the US team in a great game with the second best player being Nicolas Batum. Wemby is playoff ready. So is Fox, Kornet and Barnes. More importantly the Spurs organization is ready.

text: The Spurs are significantly better than a typical 4th seed in every metric: offense, defense, win total, net rating, and their best player is an MVP candidate and DPOY. They could lose to the Nuggets or Thunder but that is the highest bar in the league.
label: analysis

text: Writing them off strictly because they're young is lazy. They have actual faults like any other team. What completely unrelated players have done in the past shouldn't hold as much weight.
